depth.text: ampere
NVIDIA Ampere A100 SXM4: 80 GB HBM2e at 2.0 TB/s, 108 SMs, NVLink 3 at 600 GB/s per GPU
The A100 SXM4 delivers 312 TFLOPS FP16 dense (624 TFLOPS with structured sparsity) from 108 streaming multiprocessors on GA100, fabricated on TSMC 7nm with 54 billion transistors.

The A100 80GB SXM4 carries HBM2e at 2,039 GB/s (~2.0 TB/s), double V100's bandwidth, and introduces TF32 at 312 TFLOPS dense (156 TFLOPS in official docs for some table revisions) and FP64 Tensor at 19.5 TFLOPS. NVLink 3 delivers 600 GB/s per GPU versus 300 GB/s on V100, enabling all-to-all communication across 8-GPU HGX baseboards via NVSwitch. At 312 TFLOPS FP16 dense and 2.0 TB/s bandwidth, the arithmetic intensity ridge point is 312e12 / 2e12 = 156 FLOP/byte - most LLM decode operations fall below this, making A100 memory-bandwidth bound on large-batch inference.
hopper
NVIDIA Hopper H100 SXM5: 80 GB HBM3 at 3.35 TB/s, 132 SMs, NVLink 4 at 900 GB/s per GPU

The H100 SXM5 delivers 989 TFLOPS FP16 dense (1,979 TFLOPS sparse) and 1,979 TFLOPS FP8 dense (3,958 TFLOPS sparse) via fourth-generation Tensor Cores with the new Transformer Engine; FP8 is a capability absent from A100.

H100 SXM5 uses GH100 on TSMC 4N (4 nm class) with 80 billion transistors; 132 of the die's 144 SMs are enabled. Memory bandwidth jumps from A100's 2.0 TB/s to 3.35 TB/s (1.67x), and NVLink 4 doubles per-GPU bandwidth from 600 GB/s to 900 GB/s. The new FP8 Transformer Engine allows up to 9x faster LLM training over A100 by fusing attention in a dedicated hardware block. Ridge point at FP16 dense: 989e12 / 3.35e12 ~ 295 FLOP/byte - larger models with reuse push many workloads above this, making H100 more compute-bound than A100 for the same batch sizes.
NVIDIA H200 SXM: same Hopper compute die as H100 but 141 GB HBM3e at 4.8 TB/s, NVLink 4 at 900 GB/s per GPU

The H200 replaces H100's 80 GB HBM3 with 141 GB HBM3e, a 76% capacity increase and 43% bandwidth increase (3.35 TB/s to 4.8 TB/s); compute FLOPS are identical to H100 SXM5 since the GH100 die is unchanged.

H200's 4.8 TB/s bandwidth lowers the arithmetic intensity ridge point from H100's ~295 to ~206 FLOP/byte (at FP16 dense 989 TFLOPS), making a larger fraction of workloads compute-bound and improving throughput for memory-starved operations like large-context inference. The 141 GB capacity (versus 80 GB on H100) allows serving 70B-parameter models in full FP16 without tensor-parallelism across GPUs, reducing inter-GPU traffic and latency.
blackwell
NVIDIA Blackwell B200: 192 GB HBM3e at 8.0 TB/s, dual-die GB100, NVLink 5 at 1.8 TB/s per GPU

The B200 delivers 9,000 TFLOPS FP4 dense (18,000 TFLOPS sparse) and 4,500 TFLOPS FP8 dense via fifth-generation Tensor Cores; FP4 is a new precision absent from Hopper that doubles FP8 throughput and halves model memory footprint.

B200 packages two reticle-limited GB100 dies connected by a 10 TB/s NV-HBI chip-to-chip link; combined transistor count is 208 billion on TSMC 4NP. Memory bandwidth doubles Hopper H100 (2.39x: 8.0 vs 3.35 TB/s) and NVLink 5 doubles H100's NVLink 4 bandwidth (1.8 TB/s vs 900 GB/s). At FP4 dense (9,000 TFLOPS) and 8.0 TB/s, the ridge point is 1,125 FLOP/byte - frontier LLM prefill with weight reuse sits well above this, making B200 compute-bound on FP4 inference, unlike H100 where decode is bandwidth-bound.
nvlink
NVLink is NVIDIA's high-bandwidth GPU-to-GPU interconnect: gen 3 (A100) 600 GB/s, gen 4 (H100/H200) 900 GB/s, gen 5 (B200) 1.8 TB/s per GPU
NVLink 5 on B200 delivers 1.8 TB/s bidirectional per GPU - over 14x PCIe Gen5 bandwidth - and NVSwitch enables a 130 TB/s aggregate domain across all 72 GPUs in a GB200 NVL72 rack.

NVLink generations track GPU generations: NVLink 3 on A100 SXM4 provides 600 GB/s, doubling V100's 300 GB/s; NVLink 4 on H100/H200 SXM raises this to 900 GB/s; NVLink 5 on B200 reaches 1.8 TB/s. These per-GPU bandwidths set the all-reduce ceiling for tensor-parallel training - a 72-GPU GB200 NVL72 ring would need NVLink's 1.8 TB/s per GPU to prevent gradient synchronization from dominating compute at large model widths. NVSwitch, a dedicated switching chip co-packaged on HGX/HGX+ baseboards, provides the non-blocking fabric that turns point-to-point NVLink into a fully connected mesh.
gb200
GB200 NVL72: rack-scale unit of 72 Blackwell GPUs and 36 Grace CPUs in a single liquid-cooled NVLink domain delivering 1.44 exaFLOPS FP4 sparse

The GB200 NVL72 rack holds 13.4 TB of HBM3e across 72 B200 GPUs (~186 GB per GPU in the NVL72 configuration) with 576 TB/s aggregate memory bandwidth and a 130 TB/s NVLink interconnect domain - enabling it to serve a 1-trillion-parameter model without inter-rack communication.

Each GB200 compute tray pairs two B200 GPUs with one Grace CPU via NVLink-C2C at 900 GB/s, avoiding PCIe latency for CPU-GPU transfers. At 1,440 PFLOPS FP4 sparse aggregate, the NVL72 rack delivers roughly 30x the LLM inference throughput of an equivalent H100 rack per NVIDIA benchmarks. The 576 TB/s aggregate memory bandwidth means the average per-GPU bandwidth (8 TB/s) is maintained at full rack scale - no bandwidth tax for scaling from 1 to 72 GPUs within the NVLink domain.
